content.text: In Microsoft Entra ID, an Enterprise Application is the administrative view of a service principal — the local identity object that represents an application within a specific tenant. Every application that users sign in to or that accesses tenant resources has a corresponding service principal. The Entra portal surfaces these as Enterprise Applications, where you control SSO, user assignment, provisioning, and permissions.

The relationship between App Registrations and Enterprise Applications is a one-to-many projection: one App Registration (in the home tenant) generates one service principal per tenant where the app is consented. Multi-tenant SaaS products therefore have thousands of service principals across customer tenants while the App Registration exists only once.

attrs.alt: Enterprise Application model: App Registration vs Service Principal, SSO methods

content.text: You can add an enterprise application from the Entra gallery (pre-integrated SSO templates), from your own app registrations, or as a non-gallery application that you configure manually. Gallery apps include configuration hints for attribute mapping and certificate rotation.

# List available gallery applications matching a name
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Application.Read.All'

# Search the gallery (applicationTemplates)
$templates = Get-MgApplicationTemplate -Filter "contains(displayName,'Salesforce')" -ConsistencyLevel eventual
$templates | Select-Object Id, DisplayName, Categories, SupportedSingleSignOnModes

# Instantiate a gallery app from its template
$body = @{
    displayName = 'Salesforce Production'
}
$result = Invoke-MgInstantiateApplicationTemplate -ApplicationTemplateId $templates[0].Id -BodyParameter $body
# Returns both the App Registration and Service Principal (Enterprise App)
$result.Application.Id      # App Registration object ID
$result.ServicePrincipal.Id # Service Principal / Enterprise App object ID

# Or add a non-gallery enterprise app directly as a service principal
$sp = New-MgServicePrincipal -DisplayName 'Legacy HR Portal' -AccountEnabled $true
# Then configure SSO type manually
Update-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $sp.Id -PreferredSingleSignOnMode 'saml'
Legacy on-prem apps, custom SAML integrations

SSO configuration is the primary reason to manage an enterprise application. Entra ID supports four SSO modes: SAML/WS-Fed for standards-based federation, OIDC for modern cloud apps, Password SSO for credential vaulting into legacy apps, and Linked SSO for apps already protected by another system.

SAML SSO requires configuring the Basic SAML Configuration (Entity ID, Reply URL/ACS, Sign-on URL) and mapping attributes from the Entra ID user object to SAML claims. The signing certificate must be provisioned and its thumbprint shared with the service provider.

# Configure Basic SAML settings on a service principal
$spId = '<service-principal-object-id>'

# Set the identifier (Entity ID) and reply URL (ACS)
$webConfig = @{
    identifierUris = @('https://app.contoso.com/saml')
}
Update-MgApplication -ApplicationId '<app-reg-id>' -Web $webConfig

# On the service principal set reply URLs and SP-initiated login URL
Update-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $spId `
    -ReplyUrls @('https://app.contoso.com/auth/saml/callback') `
    -LoginUrl 'https://app.contoso.com/login' `
    -PreferredSingleSignOnMode 'saml'

# List SAML signing certificates
Get-MgServicePrincipalTokenSigningCertificate -ServicePrincipalId $spId |
    Select-Object DisplayName, EndDateTime, Thumbprint, Status

# Add a new self-signed certificate (valid 3 years)
$cert = Add-MgServicePrincipalTokenSigningCertificate -ServicePrincipalId $spId `
    -DisplayName 'CN=Contoso SAML Signing' `
    -EndDateTime (Get-Date).AddYears(3)
Write-Output "New cert thumbprint: $($cert.Thumbprint)"

By default, any user in the tenant can access an enterprise application. Setting 'Assignment required' to true restricts access to explicitly assigned users and groups. This gate applies before SSO and provisioning — unassigned users receive an AADSTS50105 error at sign-in.

# Require assignment before users can access the app
Update-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $spId -AppRoleAssignmentRequired $true

# Assign a user to the app (default app role)
$userId = (Get-MgUser -Filter "userPrincipalName eq 'alice@contoso.com'").Id
$defaultRoleId = '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'  # empty GUID = default role

New-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignedTo `
    -ServicePrincipalId $spId `
    -PrincipalId $userId `
    -ResourceId $spId `
    -AppRoleId $defaultRoleId

# Assign a group (members inherit access)
$groupId = (Get-MgGroup -Filter "displayName eq 'HR-Users'").Id
New-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignedTo `
    -ServicePrincipalId $spId `
    -PrincipalId $groupId `
    -ResourceId $spId `
    -AppRoleId $defaultRoleId

# List all assignments
Get-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignedTo -ServicePrincipalId $spId |
    Select-Object PrincipalDisplayName, PrincipalType, CreatedDateTime

Groups assigned to an app do NOT automatically appear as group claims in the token unless you configure 'Groups claim' in the app's token configuration. Assignment is access control; group claims are authorization data.

Entra ID's provisioning service pushes user identity data to applications via SCIM 2.0. When you enable provisioning, Entra periodically syncs users (create, update, disable, delete) to the app's SCIM endpoint using the credentials you configure. Gallery apps with pre-built connectors require only an admin token.

# Enable and configure provisioning on an enterprise app
# Provisioning uses the synchronization API in Microsoft Graph

# Step 1: Get the provisioning job template for the service principal
$jobs = Get-MgServicePrincipalSynchronizationJob -ServicePrincipalId $spId

if (-not $jobs) {
    # Create a provisioning job from the template
    $templates = Get-MgServicePrincipalSynchronizationTemplate -ServicePrincipalId $spId
    $job = New-MgServicePrincipalSynchronizationJob `
        -ServicePrincipalId $spId `
        -TemplateId $templates[0].Id
} else {
    $job = $jobs[0]
}

# Step 2: Set credentials (SCIM endpoint + bearer token)
$creds = @(
    @{ Key = 'BaseAddress'; Value = 'https://app.contoso.com/scim/v2/' },
    @{ Key = 'SecretToken'; Value = '<provisioning-bearer-token>' }
)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PUT `
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals/$spId/synchronization/secrets" `
    -Body (@{ value = $creds } | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5)

# Step 3: Validate connectivity
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST `
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals/$spId/synchronization/jobs/$($job.Id)/validateCredentials" `
    -Body '{}'

# Step 4: Start provisioning
Start-MgServicePrincipalSynchronizationJob -ServicePrincipalId $spId -SynchronizationJobId $job.Id

# Check provisioning status
$status = Get-MgServicePrincipalSynchronizationJob -ServicePrincipalId $spId -SynchronizationJobId $job.Id
$status.Status | Select-Object LastSuccessfulExecutionWithExports, Progress
Sync only assigned users and groups
Only users/groups with app role assignments are provisioned (recommended)
All tenant users provisioned; assignment still controls sign-in access
Provision a single user immediately via portal or Graph API for testing
Permissions and Consent on Enterprise Applications

The permissions panel of an enterprise application shows OAuth2 delegated permissions and application permissions that have been granted. Admin consent grants apply tenant-wide; user consent grants apply per-user. You can review and revoke grants programmatically.

# List all OAuth2 permission grants for the service principal
# Delegated permissions (user consented or admin consented on behalf of users)
Get-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -Filter "clientId eq '$spId'" |
    Select-Object ConsentType, PrincipalId, Scope, ExpiryTime

# Application role assignments (app permissions, no user context)
Get-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignment -ServicePrincipalId $spId |
    Select-Object AppRoleId, ResourceDisplayName, CreatedDateTime

# Grant admin consent for a specific delegated scope
$resourceSp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000'"  # Graph
$grant = @{
    clientId    = $spId
    consentType = 'AllPrincipals'  # tenant-wide
    resourceId  = $resourceSp.Id
    scope       = 'User.Read Mail.Read'
}
New-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -BodyParameter $grant

# Revoke all delegated grants for this service principal
Get-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -Filter "clientId eq '$spId'" |
    ForEach-Object { Remove-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -OAuth2PermissionGrantId $_.Id }

Revoking permission grants does not immediately invalidate existing access tokens. Tokens remain valid until expiry (typically 1 hour). To force immediate revocation, also revoke the user's refresh token: Revoke-MgUserSignInSession -UserId <userId>.

Enterprise applications appear in the My Apps portal (myapps.microsoft.com) for assigned users. You can control visibility, the launch URL, and the logo displayed. Applications hidden from My Apps are still accessible via direct link but not listed in the portal.

# Control visibility in My Apps portal
Update-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $spId `
    -Tags @('HideApp')  # Hide from My Apps and O365 app launcher

# Remove the HideApp tag to make it visible again
$sp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $spId
$newTags = $sp.Tags | Where-Object { $_ -ne 'HideApp' }
Update-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $spId -Tags $newTags

# Set a custom launch URL (overrides SSO-configured URL)
Update-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $spId `
    -LoginUrl 'https://app.contoso.com/dashboard'

# Upload a logo (PNG, max 215x215px, 100KB)
$logoBytes = [System.IO.File]::ReadAllBytes('logo.png')
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PUT `
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals/$spId/logo" `
    -ContentType 'image/png' `
    -Body $logoBytes

# Bulk report: list all enterprise apps visible in My Apps with assignment counts
Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "tags/any(t:t eq 'WindowsAzureActiveDirectoryIntegratedApp')" -All |
    Where-Object { 'HideApp' -notin $_.Tags } |
    Select-Object DisplayName, LoginUrl, AppRoleAssignmentRequired

The sign-in logs under an enterprise application show authentication attempts, the SSO mode used, the user agent, conditional access outcome, and error codes. This is the primary debugging surface for SSO failures.

# Query sign-in logs for a specific enterprise application (last 7 days)
$since = (Get-Date).AddDays(-7).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')

# Interactive sign-ins
Get-MgAuditLogSignIn `
    -Filter "appId eq '<client-app-id>' and createdDateTime ge $since" `
    -Top 100 |
    Select-Object CreatedDateTime, UserPrincipalName, Status, ConditionalAccessStatus,
                  @{N='ErrorCode';E={$_.Status.ErrorCode}},
                  @{N='FailureReason';E={$_.Status.FailureReason}}

# Non-interactive sign-ins (service-to-service, MSAL silent tokens)
Get-MgAuditLogSignIn -Filter "appId eq '<client-app-id>' and isInteractive eq false" -Top 50

# Summarize failure codes to identify patterns
Get-MgAuditLogSignIn -Filter "appId eq '<client-app-id>' and status/errorCode ne 0" -Top 500 |
    Group-Object { $_.Status.ErrorCode } |
    Sort-Object Count -Descending |
    Select-Object Name, Count,
        @{N='SampleReason';E={$_.Group[0].Status.FailureReason}} |
    Format-Table -AutoSize
User not assigned to the application
Add user/group assignment or disable assignment requirement
Add exact ACS/redirect URI to the app's reply URLs
Review Conditional Access session controls or SAML AuthnContext
Grant admin consent or configure pre-authorized applications

Deleting an enterprise application removes the service principal from the tenant. If the app is multi-tenant, it can be re-consented to recreate the service principal. Deleted service principals enter a soft-delete state for 30 days and can be restored. The associated App Registration is not deleted — only the service principal for this tenant.

# Soft-delete a service principal (enterprise app)
Remove-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $spId

# List soft-deleted service principals (restorable for 30 days)
Get-MgDirectoryDeletedItemAsServicePrincipal -All |
    Where-Object { $_.DeletedDateTime -gt (Get-Date).AddDays(-30) } |
    Select-Object DisplayName, AppId, DeletedDateTime

# Restore a deleted service principal
$deletedSpId = '<object-id-from-above>'
Restore-MgDirectoryDeletedItem -DirectoryObjectId $deletedSpId

# Permanently delete (cannot be restored after this)
Remove-MgDirectoryDeletedItem -DirectoryObjectId $deletedSpId

# Re-consent a multi-tenant app to recreate its service principal
# Direct users to the admin consent URL:
$appClientId = '<multi-tenant-app-client-id>'
$redirectUri = 'https://entra.microsoft.com'
$consentUrl = "https://login.microsoftonline.com/common/adminconsent" +
    "?client_id=$appClientId&redirect_uri=$([uri]::EscapeDataString($redirectUri))"
Write-Output "Admin consent URL: $consentUrl"

Conditional Access policies can target specific enterprise applications. Policies evaluate at every token issuance — even for silent refresh requests. Assigning CA policies directly to enterprise apps lets you enforce different controls per application risk level: require MFA only for high-sensitivity apps, restrict sign-in locations for admin portals, or mandate compliant devices for corporate data access.
Any internet-accessible app handling sensitive data
Apps accessing regulated data (HR, finance, legal)
Restrict to Intune-managed mobile clients for M365 apps
Apps that no longer need IMAP/SMTP/basic auth clients
Force re-authentication after N hours for high-value apps
Disable 'stay signed in' on shared workstations
Target CA policies at the application's client ID (appId), not its service principal object ID. The client ID is stable across tenants; the object ID is tenant-local.

Application Proxy publishes on-premises web applications through Entra ID without opening inbound firewall ports. A lightweight connector agent installed on a server in the corporate network establishes an outbound connection to the Entra Application Proxy service; inbound user traffic is proxied through this tunnel. Users authenticate with Entra ID (supporting MFA and Conditional Access) before reaching the on-premises app.
Users authenticate with Entra before reaching app; MFA/CA enforced
No Entra auth; connector forwards request directly to app
Apps with their own auth that would conflict with pre-auth

As tenant size grows, manual review of enterprise applications becomes impractical. The following script provides an audit report covering apps with expiring credentials, apps with excessive permissions, apps with no recent sign-in activity, and apps missing assignment controls.

# Enterprise Application governance audit report
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Application.Read.All','AuditLog.Read.All',
    'Policy.Read.All','Directory.Read.All'

# Collect all enterprise apps (service principals with app integration tag)
$allSPs = Get-MgServicePrincipal `
    -Filter "tags/any(t:t eq 'WindowsAzureActiveDirectoryIntegratedApp')" `
    -Property 'id,displayName,appId,appRoleAssignmentRequired,loginUrl,tags,keyCredentials,passwordCredentials' `
    -All

Write-Output "=== APPS WITHOUT ASSIGNMENT REQUIRED ==="
$allSPs | Where-Object { -not $_.AppRoleAssignmentRequired } |
    Select-Object DisplayName, AppId | Format-Table

Write-Output ''
Write-Output '=== EXPIRING CREDENTIALS (next 60 days) ==='
$threshold = (Get-Date).AddDays(60)
$allSPs | ForEach-Object {
    $sp = $_
    # Check password credentials (secrets)
    $sp.PasswordCredentials | Where-Object { $_.EndDateTime -lt $threshold } |
        Select-Object @{N='App';E={$sp.DisplayName}},
                      @{N='CredType';E={'Secret'}},
                      @{N='Expires';E={$_.EndDateTime}},
                      @{N='Hint';E={$_.Hint}}
    # Check key credentials (certificates)
    $sp.KeyCredentials | Where-Object { $_.EndDateTime -lt $threshold } |
        Select-Object @{N='App';E={$sp.DisplayName}},
                      @{N='CredType';E={'Certificate'}},
                      @{N='Expires';E={$_.EndDateTime}},
                      @{N='Hint';E={$_.DisplayName}}
} | Format-Table

Write-Output ''
Write-Output '=== STALE APPS (no sign-in in 90 days) ==='
$since = (Get-Date).AddDays(-90).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')
$activeAppIds = (Get-MgAuditLogSignIn `
    -Filter "createdDateTime ge $since" -Top 5000 |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty AppId -Unique)

$allSPs | Where-Object { $_.AppId -notin $activeAppIds } |
    Select-Object DisplayName, AppId |
    Format-Table

Enterprise Applications are the tenant-local service principal objects that represent applications in Microsoft Entra ID. Understanding the App Registration → Service Principal relationship is foundational to configuring SSO, user assignment, provisioning, and permissions correctly.
Enterprise Application = Service Principal; App Registration exists only in the home tenant
Four SSO modes: SAML (standards federation), OIDC (modern), Password (credential vaulting), Linked (redirect only)
Set AppRoleAssignmentRequired = true to restrict access; unassigned users get AADSTS50105
SCIM provisioning syncs identities to apps; scope to assigned users/groups to avoid over-provisioning
Admin consent grants apply tenant-wide; user consent grants are per-user and per-scope
HideApp tag removes the app from My Apps portal without blocking sign-in
Sign-in logs are the primary debugging surface; AADSTS error codes identify the failure category
Deleted service principals are soft-deleted for 30 days; multi-tenant apps can be re-consented to recreate them
